title: Q&A: Europe’s May and June heatwave deaths – and how they were counted - Carbon Brief
author: Cecilia Keating
gist: A methodological deep-dive into how scientists and public-health agencies tallied Europe's May/June 2026 heatwave deaths -- France 2,025-2,700+, Germany 5,753, Netherlands 533-577, and 10,650 excess deaths across 27 European countries -- explaining the 'excess deaths' vs 'statistical modelling' approaches, their respective biases, and the emerging practice of attributing a specific share of deaths to human-caused climate change (59% for UK's May heatwave, 38% for June).

themes.theme: Scale of Europe's 2026 heatwave death toll

insights.claim: Official and academic estimates found France recorded 2,025 excess deaths (Public Health France, 22-27 June) to over 2,700-2,766 heat-related deaths via statistical modelling over a longer 17-18 day window in June 2026, while Germany's federal statistics agency counted 5,753 excess deaths and the EuroMoMo monitoring system estimated 10,650 excess deaths across 27 European countries during the same period.
insights.speaker: Cecilia Keating
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of Europe's 2026 heat mortality crisis, a leading indicator of rising climate-driven public-health and healthcare-system cost pressure across the EU.
insights.locator: How established is the science of calculating heat deaths?

insights.claim: Separate UK research (Imperial College, LSHTM, Met Office) estimated 2,700 heat-related deaths across England and Wales during the May and June 2026 heatwaves, of which climate scientists attributed 59% of May deaths and 38% of June deaths to human-caused climate change.
insights.speaker: Cecilia Keating
entities: London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine
insights.so_what: Provides an emerging quantified 'attributable fraction' framework that could be used in litigation, insurance pricing and policy cost-benefit analysis tying specific mortality numbers to fossil-fuel-driven warming.
insights.locator: What do the latest figures show for Europe's May and June heatwaves?

insights.claim: Germany's federal statistics agency Destatis recorded 23,932 deaths in the week of 22-28 June 2026 versus an average of 18,179 for that calendar week across 2022-25, a 32% increase implying 5,753 excess deaths.
insights.speaker: Cecilia Keating
insights.so_what: A concrete national-level mortality spike figure investors/insurers can use to benchmark heatwave severity and potential claims/cost impact for German health and life-insurance exposure.
insights.locator: What are the pros and cons of the 'excess deaths' method?

insights.claim: There are two dominant methods for counting heat deaths: the 'excess deaths' approach (comparing all-cause mortality in a period to a historical baseline, used rapidly by public-health agencies in Belgium, France, Germany and the Netherlands) and the 'statistical modelling' approach (building a temperature-mortality relationship from long-term data, used by academic teams to isolate the heat-specific and climate-change-attributable death count).
insights.speaker: Cecilia Keating
insights.so_what: Establishes a reusable framework investors and analysts can apply to interpret future heat-mortality headlines and judge which figures are more rigorous versus rapid-but-noisy estimates.
insights.locator: What are the different approaches to counting heat deaths?

insights.claim: Dr Christopher Callahan (Indiana University) says the 'two-step' method of estimating heatwave deaths and then attributing a share to climate change has 'really exploded' in the last five to ten years because it requires combined epidemiological modelling and counterfactual climate analysis expertise that only recently became interdisciplinary-accessible.
insights.speaker: Cecilia Keating
insights.so_what: Signals a maturing scientific infrastructure for climate-attributed mortality claims, which underpins the growing body of evidence usable in climate litigation against fossil-fuel companies.
insights.quote: It is really only in the last five to 10 years that we have seen this, partly because it does require interdisciplinary expertise.
insights.locator: How established is the science of calculating heat deaths?

insights.claim: Dr Garyfallos Konstantinoudis (Imperial College Grantham Institute) says there is 'no ground truth' for heat-related deaths because heat is rarely listed as the primary cause on death certificates, making all heat-mortality estimates model-dependent, similar to the historical evolution of air-pollution mortality science.
insights.speaker: Cecilia Keating
insights.so_what: Explains why headline heat-death figures will always carry methodological uncertainty -- useful context for skepticism when single point-estimates are cited in policy or litigation debates.
insights.quote: We don't know what the heat-related deaths are, so we rely on different models to describe the picture.
insights.locator: What are the different approaches to counting heat deaths?

insights.claim: The excess-deaths method is fast and uses real official mortality data (which the public trusts more) but cannot isolate what fraction of deaths were actually heat-related, as shown when the WHO's 2022 estimate of 195,000 German pandemic excess deaths was revised down first to 122,000 and then to 102,000 after demographic-assumption errors were identified.
insights.speaker: Cecilia Keating
insights.so_what: A concrete historical example (WHO Covid excess-deaths revision) showing that headline excess-mortality figures can be revised by 40-45%, a caution for anyone using early heat-death numbers as fixed inputs.
insights.quote: Covid taught us that it is complicated. Depending on the different assumptions used in the excess-mortality approach, you get different results.
insights.locator: What are the pros and cons of the 'excess deaths' method?

insights.claim: The statistical-modelling method is more refined and allows attribution of a specific death count to climate change by comparing a factual heatwave against a modelled counterfactual world without warming, but depends on more assumptions (baseline years, death-toll lag periods, adaptation effects) and needs cross-checking against the excess-deaths approach.
insights.speaker: Cecilia Keating
entities: London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine
insights.so_what: Clarifies the analytical trade-off between speed/trust (excess deaths) and precision/attribution capability (statistical modelling) -- relevant for evaluating the evidentiary strength behind climate litigation claims.
insights.quote: What statistical modelling can bring is that it is more refined. It can link specific temperatures to specific impacts rather than just looking at the event [in the whole].
insights.locator: What are the pros and cons of the 'statistical modelling' method?

insights.claim: Climate-sceptic commentators and outlets (GB News, citing Oxford academics) have publicly disputed the UK's 2,700 heat-death estimate, arguing it is not reflected in provisional 'all-mortality' data from the UK Office for National Statistics and that modelled estimates should be treated with caution versus raw surveillance data.
insights.speaker: Cecilia Keating
insights.so_what: Signals an active political and media contest over heat-mortality attribution figures in the UK that could shape policy responsiveness (e.g., adaptation funding, workplace heat rules) and public perception ahead of the UKHSA's official annual report.
insights.quote: Modelling tells us nothing. Models explore possibilities; surveillance tells us what happened. When the two disagree, our instinct should be to investigate the data rather than simply trust the model.
insights.locator: What are the pros and cons of the 'statistical modelling' method?

insights.claim: Imperial's Konstantinoudis defends the modelling approach behind the UK's 2,700 estimate, noting it has 'consistently delivered comparable results' to the UK Health Security Agency's own official analysis of observed deaths in past heat events, and that studies comparing the two UK methods have found broadly similar results.
insights.speaker: Cecilia Keating
insights.so_what: Provides a credibility counterweight to sceptic pushback, suggesting the modelled UK heat-death figures are likely to hold up against the forthcoming official UKHSA report.
insights.quote: While we are entirely clear that our current findings are modelled estimates, this methodology has consistently delivered comparable results to the UKHSA's own official analyses of observed deaths for past heat events.
insights.locator: What are the pros and cons of the 'statistical modelling' method?

insights.claim: The relationship between heat and mortality has been studied for nearly a century, dating to a 1923 study by Ellsworth Huntington and Margaret Justin on 1882-88 New York City mortality data showing deaths rose rapidly above 17C, while the first modern climate-attribution mortality study (2016, Environmental Research Letters) found 506 of 735 Paris deaths and 64 of 315 London deaths in the 2003 European heatwave were attributable to human climate influence.
insights.speaker: Cecilia Keating
insights.so_what: Establishes the long scientific lineage underpinning current attribution claims, reinforcing their credibility as an evidentiary basis for climate litigation and policy.
insights.locator: How established is the science of calculating heat deaths?

insights.claim: Research published in 2025 in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences found French mortality rates follow a U-shaped curve relative to temperature, rising sharply as daily maximum temperatures depart from an optimum of approximately 20C.
insights.speaker: Cecilia Keating
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete, reusable temperature threshold (20C optimum) that underlies mortality models -- useful reference point for adaptation planning and heat-related health cost forecasting.
insights.locator: What are the pros and cons of the 'statistical modelling' method?

top_entities: London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine
